title: Expectation values after an integrable boundary quantum quench
authors: Zoltán Bajnok, Dávid Fülepi, Máté Lencsés
abstract: We investigate an integrable boundary quench, in which one integrable boundary condition is suddenly switched to another. We develop a general framework for analyzing the resulting real-time dynamics based on form factors of bulk and boundary-changing operators. We first study the problem at the conformal point of the Lee-Yang model and then extend the analysis to its massive perturbation, where we examine the time evolution of the pre-quench vacuum and compute the vacuum-to-vacuum matrix elements of local operators inserted after the quench. The analytical results are validated by numerical calculations using the truncated conformal space approach adapted to boundary-changing situations.
summary: This paper establishes a mathematical framework to calculate the real-time evolution of bulk operators after an integrable boundary quench. By studying the Lee-Yang model, the authors demonstrate how particle interference leads to specific decay laws and provide a method to reconcile numerical TCSA results with continuum field theory.
main_problem: The paper investigates the real-time dynamics and expectation values of bulk fields following an integrable boundary quantum quench, where one boundary condition is suddenly switched to another.
main_result: The authors develop a general framework using form factor expansions to calculate post-quench vacuum-to-vacuum matrix elements and identify a modified $t^{-1}$ decay law due to destructive interference in the massive theory.
method: The study employs a double form-factor expansion approach, conformal mapping to the Upper Half Plane, and the Truncated Conformal Space Approach (TCSA) for numerical validation and renormalization.
model_or_system: The primary model used is the (1+1)-dimensional Lee-Yang model, covering both its Conformal Field Theory (CFT) point and its massive integrable perturbation on a strip or semi-infinite plane.
key_observables: Expectation values (VEVs) of local bulk fields, correlation functions, and the time evolution of the vacuum amplitude.
important_parameters: Boundary conditions (identity and primary field), strip width $L$, time $t$, spatial position $x$, and the mass scale $m$.
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to integrable boundary conditions and integrable bulk perturbations, and assumes the left boundary condition remains fixed or follows specific simplified scenarios.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the physical setup and geometry mapping, 3D plots of correlation functions, energy spectra from TCSA, and comparisons between analytical predictions and numerical data including light-cone effects.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the boundary quench protocol and conformal analysis to developing the massive theory framework via form factors, followed by numerical validation using TCSA and error analysis/renormalization.
why_interesting: It provides deep insights into non-equilibrium dynamics and relaxation in integrable systems, specifically how boundary-induced excitations propagate and interfere, which is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and the study of quench-induced steady states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. We work on the strip of width L, boundary condition α or β on the left and change the right boundary condition from α to β by inserting ψβα at t = 0, and monitor the effect of the change in the bulk by inserting the operator Φ(x, t) after the change.
Figure 2. By mapping the strip to the upper half plane we arrive at the arrangements of operators.

Figure 3. 3D plot of the correlation functions ⟨¯ψIϕ|Φ(z, ¯z)ψϕI(1)|0⟩ (left) and ⟨0|Φ(z, ¯z)ψϕI(1)ψIϕ(0)|0⟩(on the right). We can recognize the change in the boundary condition at z = 1 and at z = 0, 1 respectively.

Figure 4. The correlator of the bulk field after the boundary quench in the Minkowski strip. The volume is L = 10 and we focused on the −1 ≤x ≤0 and 0 < t < 1 domain. Quenching from II to Iϕ is on the left, while from Iϕ to II is on the right.
Figure 5. Space dependence of the amplitude for different times for the quench Iϕ →II in volume L = 10. The light-cone singularity is visible for x = −t, as expected.
